entry.title: Accelerate Your Sustainability Transformation with Our New Transformation Assessment Service!
entry.content:
SAP’s sustainability portfolio enables meaningful performance targets and transformation across Sustainability Management, Climate Action, and Circular Economy. However, identifying the right solutions and building adoption readiness requires more than a product catalogue.

Without structured guidance, organizations risk misaligning their sustainability strategy with SAP solutions and missing opportunities to turn ambition into action. The new Continuous Quality Check (CQC): Transformation Assessment for Sustainability, piloted through the  SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC), provides a personalized assessment and actionable recommendations tailored to each customer's sustainability journey.

Participants receive exclusive early access to this assessment ahead of general availability, with the opportunity to directly shape the final offering through their feedback.

This pilot is included in your cloud subscription at no additional cost, all we ask in return is your honest feedback to help us improve.

Please see this teaser Video for further information.

What the Engagement Covers:

The CQC is delivered over approximately 2–3 weeks, requiring just 1–2 hours of your team's time in total. We assess your sustainability readiness and map your current state, priorities, and plans to the SAP solutions and resources that will deliver the most value. You'll receive a personalized service report, giving your organization the clarity and confidence to move forward with its sustainability transformation.

What You Bring:

  • Active project exploring SAP solutions for sustainability
  • Awareness of your sustainability strategy and priorities

What You Receive:

  • Personalised service report with key findings and actionable recommendations
  • Tailored guidance on SAP sustainability tools best suited to your needs
  • Self-consumable SAP learning resources and best practices content

Success Outcomes:

  • A clear understanding of which SAP sustainability solutions bring the most value to your business
  • A pathway to extend into additional sustainability solutions or related services

Ready to get started?

Contact us today to begin your Sustainability Transformation Assessment: esac@sap.com

entry.title: Why Your ERP Matters More in the Age of Agentic AI, Not Less
entry.content:
Agentic AI elevates the role of ERP. But only if the architecture can support what comes next.

Every enterprise running SAP has built something valuable: decades of process logic, business rules, transactional history, and institutional knowledge encoded into a system that runs the most critical operations in the company.

Now, as AI reshapes every layer of the enterprise, a question is emerging: does the ERP still matter as much, or does intelligence shift elsewhere?

The answer is unambiguous. In the age of agentic AI, AI does not merely recommend but acts. Your ERP matters more than it ever has. It holds the process context, the data semantics, and the governance infrastructure that agentic AI needs to operate reliably. Without it, AI can suggest. With it, AI can execute.

But here is the critical point: the ERP can only serve as the foundation for agentic AI if its architecture is designed for it. The investment you have made over decades is the accelerator. The question is whether the architecture enables what comes next.

ERP Is Where the Consequential Decisions Happen 

The highest-value AI in your enterprise will not draft emails or summarize meetings. It will operate where the most consequential decisions are made: financial close, supply chain optimization, procurement execution, workforce planning.

These processes live in ERP. They have always lived in ERP. And in an AI-driven enterprise, they do not move somewhere else; they become intelligent where they are.

For AI to act and execute reliably in these processes, it needs three things:

Process context. An AI agent managing your financial close must understand the full lifecycle: what triggered the process, what dependencies exist, what exceptions have occurred historically, what the downstream implications of each action are. This intelligence lives in ERP and nowhere else.

Institutional knowledge. Every enterprise has decades of business-specific decisions encoded in its systems: how exceptions are handled, which approvals are required under which conditions, what tolerances apply in which markets. Without this context, AI operates on generic assumptions, which is unacceptable for core operations.

Governance infrastructure. When AI acts autonomously within your financial reconciliation or procurement workflow, it must operate within clear boundaries: audit trails, authorization controls, compliance guardrails. This is the difference between AI the business trusts and AI the auditor rejects.

No other system holds all three. ERP is where agentic AI must live.

Core Processes Require Process-Native AI 

Consider what happens when an AI agent posts a journal entry that is off by a fraction of a cent. In isolation, trivial. Across thousands of transactions in a regulated financial close, it creates reconciliation failures, audit findings, and restatement risk. 

Or consider procurement: an AI agent that recommends a supplier based on a "best guess" rather than contractual terms, compliance status, and real-time inventory creates liability, not value. 

These are not edge cases. They represent the fundamental standard that core business processes demand: deterministic accuracy. Financial reconciliation must balance to the penny. Supply chain commitments must reflect actual constraints. Procurement decisions must stand up to audit. 

The AI built into SAP is designed for this standard. It delivers deterministic, auditable outcomes grounded in real transactional data, constrained by actual business rules, and validated against the process logic that governs your operations. It does not guess at what your data means; it knows, because it operates within the system that created and maintains that data. 

This is an architectural distinction, not a marketing one. Process-native AI understands the relationships between your transactional tables, the dependencies between process steps, and the governance rules that constrain what is permissible. That structural understanding is what makes the difference between AI that can advise and AI that can act. 

What Makes AI from SAP Different in Core Processes

The question enterprises are asking is clear: what can AI from SAP do within my core processes that other AI cannot?

It knows how your business connects. SAP's Business AI Platform encodes fifty years of enterprise process intelligence: how procure-to-pay, order-to-cash, and design-to-operate actually work at scale. When an AI agent operates within SAP, it inherits the structural understanding of how your processes interconnect. A third-party model can query your data (data is always yours). It cannot replicate this understanding of process interdependency.

Your data stays where it belongs. Enterprises with mature security postures do not expose core transactional data to external AI models. AI provided by SAP operates natively on your business data within your security boundary. There is no extraction, no external processing, no exposure of your most sensitive operational information to models you do not control.

Governance and auditability are built into the platform by design. AI Agent Hub's formal verification workflow creates an approval and ownership record for each agent, providing the audit trail needed for regulatory governance. It also logs agent sessions and captures execution traces, recording what an agent did, which tools it called, and what outcome it produced, with observability and governance capabilities being delivered in phases, intended to support audit and compliance requirements built in from the start, not added after the fact.

It reasons across the full enterprise. No single third-party AI vendor spans procure-to-pay, design-to-operate, and order-to-cash. SAP's cross-functional coverage enables AI that reasons across the enterprise, not within silos. An AI agent optimizing your supply chain understands the financial implications. An agent managing procurement sees the downstream impact on production planning.

It works with the rest of your AI landscape. AI from SAP supports the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol, enabling seamless coordination between SAP agents and third-party AI across your ecosystem. This is not a walled garden: it is a governed platform that interoperates with your broader technology landscape while maintaining the deterministic accuracy and audit-grade governance your core processes require.

 

Why Modernization Unlocks What Comes Next

This is where the modernization imperative becomes concrete.

Enterprises running ECC or on-premises S/4HANA have built a strong operational foundation, one that has run core business processes reliably for decades. Agentic AI introduces a new set of requirements on top of that foundation: real-time semantic interpretation, end-to-end process visibility, and governance frameworks built for autonomous action.

Many customers are already extending their environment with third-party AI tools to start capturing value now. That approach delivers real, if narrow, value. Because these tools sit outside the ERP's native process governance, they operate with less full process context, less audit-grade governance, and less reach across end-to-end process chains than natively embedded AI can provide.

On-premises ERP architecture was designed for a different era. It served that era well. Agentic AI asks something new of the architecture:

  • Real-time semantic data that AI can interpret without transformation
  • End-to-end process visibility without blind spots at system boundaries
  • Native governance frameworks designed for autonomous agents
  • A platform that evolves as AI evolves, with new capabilities on a predictable cadence

These aren't shortcomings in what you've built. They're the next requirements your investment is positioned to meet. Layering tools on top of an on-premises system extends what it can do today. Modernizing the architecture is what determines what it can become.

The Question Is Not Whether Your ERP Matters 

It matters more than ever. 

In the age of agentic AI, ERP becomes the platform where AI acts on your most consequential business decisions, with full context, full governance, and deterministic accuracy. No other system holds the process depth, institutional knowledge, and governance infrastructure that agentic AI requires. 

The question is whether your architecture enables that role. 

Your decades of SAP investment position you to capitalize on what comes next. The process knowledge is the fuel. The business understanding is the differentiator. Modernization is what transforms that investment into the AI-ready foundation your enterprise needs, where governed, process-native intelligence operates across the decisions that matter most. 

And the journey itself has changed. SAP now applies the same AI-first approach to the modernization process: an agent-led toolchain with seven purpose-built migration and modernization assistants that assess your landscape, map custom code, accelerate testing, and reduce overall effort by up to 35%. The path to an AI-ready ERP is now AI-accelerated. 

The gap widens every quarter. New AI agents are released on Cloud ERP, compounding advantage for organizations on modern foundations. The question is not whether to modernize. It is how quickly you want agentic AI working for you. 

Watch the webinar: See how the 7 migration and modernization assistants and agents accelerate your journey → https://event.on24.com/wcc/r/5365924/0A3E9159F7A3A127BFAA24CE52BE1084/6794304

entry.title: Engaging your workforce at every step with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding
entry.content:
Organizations invest significant time and effort helping new hires gain momentum to become productive from the start, then risk losing that momentum when employees change roles, move departments, return after time away, or leave the company.

Each critical employee transition deserves an experience that helps employees feel prepared, supported, and empowered at any stage of their career. These key moments require connecting people, processes, communications, approvals, learning, and seamless coordination to ensure productivity continues through every people change.

That’s where your onboarding solution moves beyond enabling successful new hire experiences to an engine that keeps your business running smoothly through all employee transitions. With SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, you already have the foundation to support every employee transition, unlock greater productivity, upskill talent through changes, and fuel success for both your people and your business. 

Extending the value of your SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution

Every employee transition is an opportunity to accelerate productivity, strengthen engagement, and deliver more consistent employee experiences. Yet, many organizations still rely on disconnected processes, manual coordination, and one-off communications to support these moments of change.

With SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, organizations can expand the value of their existing onboarding solution to orchestrate every employee transition with consistency from one connected, intelligent solution that engages your workforce at every career milestone. The same capabilities that help new hires succeed – personalized journeys, guided tasks, learning, consistent communications, and agentic AI-driven assistance – can also support employees through internal mobility, career growth, organizational changes, offboarding, and more.

By creating consistent, connected experiences across every transition, organizations can reduce administrative effort, keep employees productive through change, and ensure every career milestone is supported with the right guidance at the right time.

Key benefits include:

  • More consistent employee experiences across every transition
  • Less manual HR and hiring manager administration
  • Faster time to productivity during changes and transitions
  • Globally scalable, standardized processes and orchestration  
  • Improved visibility and coordination
  • Reduced HR compliance cost and mitigated risk
  • Personalized journeys that support employees through every career change

Enhance experiences across the employee lifecycle 

Similar to the hiring experience, employee transitions often require addressing many of the same needs for your workforce: helping employees quickly understand their responsibilities, upskill, complete necessary activities, connect with the right people, and become successful in their next chapter. Here are a few use cases where organizations can extend their SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution beyond the traditional new hire experience:   

  • Internal Mobility & Role Change (Crossboarding) - When employees move into new roles, departments, or business units, they need more than a title change. A thoughtful transition helps employees understand new responsibilities, complete required training, gain system access, connect with their new team, and become productive faster – while giving managers the tools to support success from day one. Instead of treating internal moves as administrative changes in the system, organizations can turn these key moments into growth experiences that enable your talent to step into new roles with the tools, resources, and support needed to thrive.
  • Promotions & Succession (Crossboarding) - A promotion is an opportunity to set employees up for long-term success. Whether someone is stepping into a leadership position or taking on new responsibilities, they often need guidance, learning, introductions, and support as they transition into their new role. By providing a tailored crossboarding experience, employees can more easily build new skills and update their profile, gain confidence, and become productive in their new role.  
  • Organizational Changes and Legal Entity Transfers (Crossboarding) - Whether an employee is relocating, changing business units, moving internationally, joining a newly acquired organization, navigating a concurrent employment or a reorganization, transitions require coordinated experiences across multiple teams. Automated workflows and mass initiation help ensure nothing is missed and compliance requirements are met, while giving employees and your business confidence throughout the process.
  • Rehire - Returning employees are already familiar with your organization and culture, but that doesn’t mean they should skip the onboarding process. Rehires often need updated policies, refreshed training, new technology access, organizational updates, and new role-specific guidance. A tailored rehire journey recognizes their previous experience while ensuring they’re set up for success in today’s organization.
  • Offboarding - The employee experience doesn’t end with a resignation. A consistent and structured offboarding process supports compliance, coordinates equipment returns and access removal, captures key feedback, creates a knowledge transfer plan, and leaves employees with a positive final impression.   
  • Alumni - Once an employee’s time at a company ends, they may quickly feel disconnected and unsure of how to access relevant documents. Maintaining an alumni experience allows organizations to continue supporting former employees with access to post-employment resources, important documents, and communications. It also helps strengthen relationships with departing employees, making it easier to engage former employees as loyal advocates, referrals, or future rehires.

Boost continuous workforce productivity with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding

SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding transforms onboarding processes into an autonomous workforce activation experience. AI agents seamlessly orchestrate workflows, anticipate needs, and ensure employees are connected, productive, and fully prepared for day one through every career milestone.

  • Start before day one – Use recruiting data to auto-generate tasks, communications, and workflows so employees arrive prepared and productive from the start.
  • Personalized journeys for every transition – Extend beyond onboarding to mobility, growth, and transitions that engage employees at every career milestone with guided experiences, tailored journeys, custom tasks, and configurable workflows.
  • Agent driven automation – Use intelligent Task Agents and embedded AI capabilities to automatically orchestrate work across HR, IT, and hiring managers; launching, assigning, and tracking tasks without manual follow-ups.
  • Connected data foundation, skills, and experiences – As part of the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite, ensure trusted data, embedded compliance, real-time insights, ongoing skills development, and a continuous employee experience.
  • Global scale, local control – Deliver consistent experiences globally while dynamically meeting local regulations and compliance requirements for any employee transition.  

Every transition is an opportunity to strengthen engagement  

Every employee transition deserves a meaningful, structured, and personalized experience that supports your people and your business. By extending your SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution to support crossboarding, rehires, transfers, organizational changes, offboarding, and alumni, organizations can create more connected employee experiences while improving operational efficiency, coordination, and continued productivity. 

Many organizations already have the capabilities to support these journeys as part of their SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution – the next step is expanding how they’re used across the entire employee lifecycle. Learn more about SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding.

entry.title: Re: #CoE#CSRI Goods movement 657E and 657 in return delivey?
entry.content:
Dear @leah2,

SAP KBA 2844101 confirms that the requirement type MSR for schedule line category AR sets the special stock indicator E. The same KBA also confirms that no configuration step is currently available in Public Cloud to change this customizing. Therefore there is no standard configuration to force FI document generation for the original 657 E goods receipt while keeping Refund Control = Decide Later

If an FI document is required immediately at goods receipt the standard option is to choose an early refund option, for example Create Credit Memo Request instead of Decide Later

If the business must keep Decide Later the supported process is to complete the refund decision later in Manage Customer Returns app -> Determine Refund -> Release Selected Items. This can generate the relevant follow-up document such as a credit memo request but it does not retroactively create an FI document for the original 657 E goods receipt

Since SAP confirms that no configuration step is available for this behavior the SAP proposed path for changing/enhancing the standard behavior is to submit a feature request through SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 2844101 – Return stock is received as a special stock
  • SAP Community Customer Returns Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM
  • SAP KBA 3765245 – No FI Document Generation in Returns Delivery in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP KBA 3093842 – No response when trying to refund an accelerated customer return in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Product

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Introducing Our New Webinar Series: Modern Identity in a Connected, High-Expectation World
entry.content:
Introducing Our New Webinar Series: Modern Identity in a Connected, High-Expectation World

Identity has quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern digital stack. It influences how customers sign up, how they log in, how they are protected, how they are understood—and ultimately, how they experience a brand.

As organizations continue to navigate rising expectations around privacy, security, and personalization, identity is no longer just a backend concern. It sits at the intersection of marketing, security, data, and customer experience.

That’s the backdrop for our upcoming webinar series focused on SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM)—a set of four focused sessions exploring how organizations are rethinking identity to drive growth, reduce risk, and simplify increasingly complex digital ecosystems.

Each session takes a deep dive into a specific challenge teams are actively working through today, from fraud and fragmentation to first-party data strategy and technical debt. And because each topic is designed to be interactive and practical, space is limited.

Below is a look at what we’ll be covering.

Session 1: Beyond Login: Turning Digital Identity into a Revenue Engine in a Cookie-Constrained World

TUESDAY, JULY 14 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

With third-party cookies disappearing and customer acquisition costs rising, organizations are looking for new ways to build reliable first-party data strategies without adding friction.

This session explores how identity can become a growth driver by enabling privacy-safe data collection, progressive profiling, and real-time consent management. Learn how to enrich customer profiles across touchpoints and improve attribution through identity-connected signals across your marketing and commerce ecosystem.

Register here

 

Session 2: Fraud, Friction, and the False Tradeoff: Designing Secure Authentication Without Killing Customer Experience

TUESDAY, JULY 28 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Fraud is increasing—but so are expectations for seamless digital experiences. This session challenges the idea that security must come at the cost of usability.

We’ll explore how adaptive, risk-based authentication can reduce fraud while keeping trusted users on a frictionless path. Topics include passkeys, device intelligence, behavioral signals, and modern authentication patterns that balance protection with conversion.

Register here

 

Session 3: Global Customers, Local Compliance: Solving Identity Fragmentation Across Regions, Brands, and Devices

TUESDAY, AUG 11 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Global organizations face growing complexity from regional regulations, brand fragmentation, and duplicate user identities across systems.

This session looks at how to unify identity across geographies while still meeting local compliance needs. Learn how to reduce fragmentation with identity linking, support data residency requirements, and enable consistent experiences across brands and channels.

Register here

 

Session 4: Identity as Infrastructure: Reducing Technical Debt While Enabling Marketing Agility

TUESDAY, AUG 25 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Many organizations still treat identity as a supporting layer, but it increasingly functions as core digital infrastructure.

This session explores how a modern CIAM foundation can centralize identity services, reduce custom integrations, and enable faster innovation across marketing, commerce, and analytics. Learn how to simplify your stack while improving agility, governance, and scalability.

Register here

Join one, or join them all. We are excited to host you

entry.title: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
There's a problem that comes up on almost every multi-project SAP BTP engagement, and I've seen teams solve it in some really messy ways.

You have two CAP projects. One of them — let's call it Project A — owns a set of shared constants, configuration parameters, maybe a job run log. Project B needs to read that data. So what do people do? They copy the table. Or they create an API. Or they hardcode values that really shouldn't be hardcoded.

All of these work. None of them are clean.

The proper solution is HDI cross-container synonyms. Once it's set up, Project B can query Project A's HANA tables directly — same SQL, same CAP service code, no replication, no drift between environments. It looks and feels like a local table. It just happens to live in someone else's HDI container.

I put together a Tech Bite on this recently and figured it's worth writing up properly, because the documentation is scattered and the first time you set it up you will almost certainly hit at least one of the three errors I mention at the end.

The thing nobody explains first: why it's complicated at all

HDI containers are isolated. That's the point of them — each CAP project gets its own schema, its own deploy lifecycle, its own access controls. But that isolation also means Container B can't just say "I want to read from Container A's schema" — because the schema name is dynamically assigned at deployment time. You can't hardcode it.

So SAP's solution is a User-Provided Service, or UPS. It's a simple Cloud Foundry construct — basically a key-value store with no runtime component — that carries the schema name of Project A. You register it with the -t hana tag, declare it in Project B's mta.yaml, and the HDI deployer picks it up during deployment and injects the correct schema wherever you've referenced it.

That's it. That's the whole trick. Everything else — the synonym files, the config files, the grants — is just plumbing on top of that core idea.

What you're actually building in Project B

Once you understand what the UPS is doing, the rest makes sense. You need four types of files:

.hdiconfig tells the deployer which plugins to use. You need two of these — one in db/src/ and one in db/cfg/. It's a small JSON file, easy to get wrong by forgetting one of the two locations.

.hdbsynonym is where you define the local alias. So instead of referencing PROJECT_A_SCHEMA.CONSTANTS_TABLE (which you can't hardcode anyway), you just write CONSTANTS_TABLE and map it to the actual technical object name in Project A. This lives in db/src/.

.hdbsynonymconfig is the file that connects the synonym to the UPS. This is what makes the schema resolution dynamic. It lives in db/cfg/ — deliberately separate from db/src/ to avoid deployment conflicts. The key thing here is that the value you put in schema.configure must start with exactly the same string you used as the key in mta.yaml. More on this when I get to the errors.

.hdbgrants is the access control piece. Project B's container needs to be explicitly granted a role from Project A. That role needs to already exist over in Project A — it's not something you create in Project B. Your Project A team needs to have a role set up that grants SELECT on the tables you need.

The setup, briefly

On the Cloud Foundry side, you create the UPS once:

cf create-user-provided-service ProjectA_UPS \
  -p '{"schema": "YOUR_PROJECT_A_SCHEMA"}' -t hana

Then in Project B's mta.yaml, you declare it as an existing service resource and bind it to the db-deployer module under SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS:

- name: ProjectA-UPS-resource
  group: SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS
  properties:
    key: ProjectA_UPS
    service: ~{service-name}

Your synonym config then references it like this:

"schema.configure": "ProjectA_UPS/schema"

And once everything deploys, your CAP service code doesn't need to know any of this happened. You just query it:

const db = await cds.connect.to("db");
const results = await db.run(
  `SELECT * FROM "CONSTANTS_TABLE" WHERE "PARAM" = ?`, [value]
);

Clean. No different from querying a local table.

The three errors you will probably hit

I'm not going to pretend this always works first time. Here's what goes wrong most often:

"synonym config refers to unknown service replacement" — This one is almost always a key mismatch. The string you put as key in mta.yaml and the prefix before /schema in your .hdbsynonymconfig need to be identical. Character for character. Check both files side by side.

"insufficient privilege" — The role you referenced in .hdbgrants doesn't exist in Project A, or it exists but doesn't include SELECT on the tables you're trying to reach. You'll need to go back to whoever owns Project A and check what roles are actually deployed there. HANA DB Explorer is your friend here.

"object not found" — The object name in your .hdbsynonym is wrong. The technical name of a table in HDI usually follows a namespace pattern with underscores replacing dots — something like HR_HTR_UTILITY_OBJECTS_CONSTANTS_TABLE. The only reliable way to find the exact name is to look it up directly in HANA DB Explorer under Project A's schema.

One thing worth knowing for local development

When you run cds watch locally, you're using SQLite — there's no HDI container, no synonym. So you need a workaround for local testing.

The simplest approach is to create a CDS entity with @CDS.persistence.exists that mirrors the external table's structure, and seed it with a CSV file. It won't be connected to Project A's live data, but it lets you develop and test the rest of your logic without needing a full BTP deployment every time.

Worth the setup

The first time you wire this up it feels like a lot of moving parts. And honestly, it is. But once it's done, it just works — and it works cleanly. No sync jobs, no API calls for what is essentially a config lookup, no risk of the two sides drifting apart over time.

If your project has shared reference data living in another team's HDI container, this is the right way to access it.

Happy to answer questions in the comments if you hit something I haven't covered here.

#SAP #SAPCAP #SAPHANA #BTP #SAPDevelopment #HDI #CloudFoundry #PwC

entry.title: From Gross Revenue to Gross Margin: A Practitioner's Guide to Margin Management
entry.content:
Margin is often explained with a simple formula: margin = revenue − cost. The reality is more nuanced, especially when you sell across multiple products, channels and regions. That’s why many organizations can see revenue quickly but still struggle to explain profitability until after the period ends.

Think of margin management as a journey: Gross Revenue → Net Revenue → Gross Margin. Each step introduces a different kind of complexity, and in SAP terms, we leverage different capabilities.

Managing the journey from revenue to margin

Figure 1 - Managing the journey from revenue to margin

Note: For simplicity, in this blog we are not considering intercompany or tax complexity

 

The three margin levels every business should distinguish

 

1) Gross Revenue

Gross revenue begins with the sales price (or invoice price). It’s typically visible immediately. But it’s rarely the true picture, because commercial programs and post-sale adjustments can change the realized revenue significantly.

2) Net Revenue

Net revenue is gross revenue after commercial conditions are applied. These include  promotions and discounts, customer rebates, billbacks, and market development funds (MDF).

This is where surprises often appear, especially with complex B2B agreements. Many of these adjustments are off‑invoice, conditional on volume or performance, and settled later. If they are not modeled and accrued accurately, net revenue is overstated and the margin looks better than it really is.

3) Gross Margin

From net revenue, the focus shifts to costs. Direct costs (COGS) are usually linkable to products. Indirect costs such as stores, contact centers, warehousing, logistics, shared services, and overhead are not.

This is where cost‑to‑serve and activity‑based costing (ABC) come into play. Indirect costs need to be allocated using operational drivers (shipments, service interactions, store footprint, etc.).

A quick example in consumer goods.

Imagine a company selling a smartphone through retail stores and direct online. Direct costs (the handset BOM, procurement, and warranty provisioning) are consistent per unit. But indirect costs behave very differently:

  • Store channel includes rent, staff, utilities and in‑store marketing. These costs are best allocated by store traffic, transactions, floor area, or staffed hours.
  • Online includes e‑commerce platform cost, payment fees, fulfillment, shipping, and returns handling—costs best allocated by orders, parcels, return rates, and handling time.

Without allocation logic, the same phone can appear to have the “same margin” across channels when the cost-to-serve is actually very different.

 

Selecting the right tool for each margin lever

SAP’s margin management portfolio maps naturally to the journey from gross revenue to net revenue, and to gross margin:

  • Margin Analysis in SAP S/4HANA provides the real-time profitability foundation for actuals with a consistent view across product, customer, channel, and region. Universal Allocation standardizes allocations and top-down distributions, so costs are aligned across these dimensions in Margin Analysis.
  • SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex support the gross‑to‑net portion. This models complex rebates and incentives (including billbacks/MDF and other off‑invoice conditions) and can simulate the net margin impact of commercial agreements before they are signed-off.
  • SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) extends SAP S/4HANA indirect costing with advanced modeling for cost allocation, cost‑to‑serve, and multi-step simulations that often require additional data and allocation logic beyond standard ERP processing.

Together, these capabilities support a simple operating model:

 

Capability

What it answers

Example outcome

Margin Analysis in SAP S/4HANA

What has happened to my margins?Real-time visibility into revenue, cost, and margin.

SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM)

Why are my margins changing?Insight into cost drivers, allocations, and profitability scenarios.

SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex

How can I improve my gross-to-net?Optimized pricing, rebates, and incentive programs.

Table 1- Complementary capabilities

 

Actuals, forecasts, and simulation: making margin actionable

Margin management also has a timing dimension. SAP S/4HANA Margin Analysis supports profitability insights during the period. Indirect costs can be allocated on a cadence that matches the business, supporting a continuous-closing mindset rather than waiting for period-end.

From there, teams look ahead: short-term rolling forecasts often require granular detail (product/customer/channel), while long-term plans are more aggregated (country/category/line of business). Margin simulation sits between these worlds: the what‑if bridge that helps teams respond quickly to change, making decisions that will keep margins tracking to forecasts.

Returning to the mobile phone example:
If transportation costs rise (due to an oil price spike or route disruption), the company needs to understand the margin impact by channel and customer agreement. Store replenishment costs may increase due to frequent shipments, while online margins may be hit harder by last‑mile delivery and returns. Modeling of both cost-to-serve and gross-to-net helps answer practical questions such as:

  • Should we introduce a shipping surcharge or raise prices selectively?
  • Should we adjust promotional funding, rebates, or MDF levels for certain partners?
  • Should we change service levels (e.g., free shipping thresholds) or reroute fulfillment to reduce cost?

These operational decisions connect commercial levers and cost levers using consistent data in SAP S/4HANA shared by each of the capabilities.

Use cases, products and primary users

Figure 2- Use cases, products and primary users

 

Conclusion

Margin is a journey from gross revenue, through gross‑to‑net adjustments, to gross margin after direct and indirect costs. SAP supports this end‑to‑end journey with a coherent set of capabilities: Margin Analysis for real-time, reconciled actuals; PaPM for advanced allocations and simulation; and Margin Optimization to manage the commercial programs that impact the gross-to-net.

 

Authors

John Fallon – Global Product Marketing Manager for Pricing, SAP

Michel Haesendonckx -  VP, Global Functional Lead – oCFO, SAP

 

Appendix – Products Referenced

SAP Margin Analysis

  • Margin Analysis is a capability in SAP S/4HANA for profitability analysis based on the Universal Journal.
  • It combines financial and management accounting data in one place and supports near real-time insight.
  • Users can monitor margin by dimensions like product, customer, or channel as transactions occur.
  • It supports continuous closing by allowing frequent allocations and updates.

Learn more

 

SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM)

  • PaPM extends SAP S/4HANA with advanced profitability modeling and cost allocation.
  • It supports activity-based costing, multi-step allocations, and scenario modeling.
  • Models can use operational drivers and supplementary data not maintained in ERP.
  • It is useful for explaining margin changes and allocating indirect costs (for example, cost-to-serve).

Product page

 

SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex

  • These solutions manage complex pricing and incentive agreements such as rebates, billbacks, and MDF.
  • Supports simulation of expected net revenue outcomes before agreements are signed.
  • The aim is to align commercial programs with finance’s view of net revenue and reduce surprises in gross-to-net.
  • Applicable where gross-to-net complexity, accrual accuracy, or rebate settlement are known pain points.

Overview page

entry.title: Resolving the Grayed-Out "Create Purchase Order" Button in Fiori's Process Purchase Requisitions App
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I wanted to share a quick workaround I recently learnt while working with a practice ad-hoc procurement scenario in SAP S/4HANA using Fiori apps.

The Scenario: When I attempted to convert an ad-hoc Purchase Requisition (created using manual short text without a pre-existing Material Master Record) into a Purchase Order using the Process Purchase Requisitions app, I found out that the Create Purchase Order button was still grayed out even after I selected the line item.

The Root Cause: The standard processing view expects a valid Source of Supply (Supplier) to be assigned at the PR level before allowing direct conversion which I did not do

The Workaround: Instead of forcing the conversion from the PR view, I navigated to the Manage Purchase Orders app:

  1. Clicked Create to launch a clean PO draft.

  2. Enter my Header Data (Supplier, Purchasing Org, etc.) manually.

  3. I Used the Add from Document function under the Items section, I filtered by my PR number, and adopted the line items directly into the draft.

This pulls all custom short texts, account assignments (e.g., Category K), and pricing smoothly without needing to wait for a database master record. Hope this helps anyone encountering the same layout block!.

I am open to any other tips as I continue my SAP MM journey.

                     Best Regards

entry.title: Re: Kerberos / SPNEGO Authentication with SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
entry.content:
Hello  @arilssonbernardi,

thank you for this great blog. It helped me a lot to get SSO working for our SAP Cloud Identity Services
Maybe one very important step I need to find out by myself.
There are also browser policys, that are important.
For example in MS Edge: (for other browser there are similar steps, but I only need it for MS Edge)

Check the following url in MS Edge: edge://policy/
There are two important attributes that are needed to set:

AuthNegotiateDelegateAllowlistSpecifies a list of servers that Microsoft Edge can delegate user credentials to
AuthServerAllowlistConfigure list of allowed authentication servers

and maybe

AuthNegotiateDelegateByKdcPolicyUse KDC policy to delegate credentials.
DisableAuthNegotiateCnameLookupDisable CNAME lookup when negotiating Kerberos authentication
EnableAuthNegotiatePortInclude non-standard port in Kerberos SPN

In my environment it was enough to configure the first two.

Regards
Stefan

Is there a way to ensure that, despite the date filter, pairs that belong together—even if one side no longer fits within the date range—are still displayed, so that no delta is shown in the Fiori Reconciliation Balances?

Note: This issue does not appear to be related to the updates introduced with Release CE2502.

entry.title: Putaway WT Creation to Consider Storage Bin capacity Including Existing Stock and Open WTs
entry.content:
Hi,

We would like to check whether the below putaway requirement can be achieved using standard SAP EWM functionality.

During automatic putaway Warehouse Task creation, the system should consider the maximum allowed weight/load capacity of a storage bin group or bay. The maximum weight of the storage group/bay load should be treated as the cumulative weight capacity of all bins assigned to that storage group/bay.

Business Requirement

During automatic WT creation for putaway, the system should evaluate each destination bin proposed during the storage bin search sequence and perform the following checks:

  1. Identify the destination storage bin proposed by the system during putaway search.
  2. Determine the storage group/bay associated with that bin.
  3. Identify all other storage bins belonging to the same storage group/bay.
  4. Check whether there are already pallets/HUs physically present in those bins.
  5. Check whether there are any open Warehouse Tasks directed to any of the bins within the same storage group/bay.
  6. Calculate the total expected load of the storage group/bay as follows:

Total Load =Current stock/HU weight already present in all bins of the storage group+ Weight of open Warehouse Tasks already directed to bins in the same storage group+ Weight of the current pallet/HU being put away

  1. Compare this calculated total load against the maintained maximum allowed weight/load capacity of the storage group/bay.

Expected System Behavior:

If the calculated total load is greater than the maximum allowed weight of the storage group/bay:

System should not create the automatic putaway Warehouse Task.An error/exception should be raised.

 If the calculated total load is less than or equal to the maximum allowed weight of the storage group/bay: 

System should allow creation of the putaway Warehouse Task. 

 This logic should be applied to each and every bin considered by the system during the putaway search sequence execution, before the system finalizes the destination bin and creates the Warehouse Task. 

 

Example Logic

If a storage group/bay has multiple bins assigned to it, the system should not only check the individual bin capacity, but should also check the overall load capacity of the complete storage group/bay.

Additionally, the system should also consider open Warehouse Tasks already created toward the bins in that same storage group/bay, because these tasks represent future expected stock/load.

Clarification Required from SAP

Could you please confirm whether this requirement can be achieved using standard SAP EWM Public cloud functionality?

Specifically, we would like to know:

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in SAP EWM to check the maximum cumulative weight of a storage bin group/bay during automatic putaway WT creation?
  2. Can standard putaway strategy consider not only the current stock in the bins, but also the open Warehouse Tasks directed to those bins while calculating available load capacity?
  3. Is there any standard enhancement/BAdI recommended by SAP to implement this logic during destination bin determination or putaway WT creation?
  4. If this is not supported in standard, could SAP please confirm whether this requirement must be handled through a custom enhancement?

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Putaway WT Creation to Consider Storage Bin capacity...
entry.content:
Hello,

 

Please go to Customizing "Maintain Storage Types", there are customizing field "Capavity Check" and Early Cap Check which can help with the requirement.

 

Below are the F1 help for the fields:

Capacity Check Method

This indicator defines the capacity check for the dimensionless capacity factor that is performed for the storage bins in this storage type, and also specifies whether the capacity check should take place at all.

Use

If you use the dimensionless capacity factor, you must specify here the data to use for the check, in order to guarantee that the limited capacity of the bins is not exceeded during putaway. In the bins or bin types, you must also enter a maximum value for the capacity factor.

The system automatically performs a capacity check for weight and volume as soon as a maximum weight or volume is entered for the bin.

In some storage types, there is no danger of overloading (for example, in the goods receipt zone). You can switch off the capacity check for all values here. In particular you should switch of the capacity check for storage types that contain your work centers.

 

Early Capacity Check for Bin Type:

 

Select this checkbox if you want to activate the early capacity check based on the storage bin type for putaway warehouse tasks in the storage type.

If the capacity of a storage bin type is insufficient, the early capacity check is used to take bins of the storage bin type from the destination bin search. This enables you to improve performance when working with a large number of storage bins, since the system does not have to check each bin individually.

If the mentioned the standard customizing cannot fulfill the requirement, below BADIs are available:

 

  • /SCWM/EX4_CORE_PTS_FILT_EMP - Bin Filtering and Sorting: Empty Bins in Putaway
  • /SCWM/EX4_CORE_PTS_FILT_STK  - Bin Filtering and Sorting: Possible Bins for Additional Stock

Best Regards,

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI 10-second connect timeout (NiBufIConnect / WSAEWOULDBLOCK) — network's clean
entry.content:
Thanks for the response! I really appreciate the input. Two things I'd love to dig into further:

On the SAP GUI version: Good point on 7.30 being long out of support — noted that we need to plan an upgrade. Quick question though: in your experience, does upgrading to a currently supported SAP GUI version (7.70 / 8.00) actually change how NiBufIConnect handles transient network jitter? My understanding is that the underlying NI layer behavior on TCP connect timeout is largely unchanged across versions, so I'm curious whether the upgrade would meaningfully reduce these popup incidents or just get us back on a supported baseline. Would love to know if newer versions have improved connection retry logic or timeout handling that would specifically help here.

On the network/firewall angle: Since my client-side probe to port 3200 shows 0% packet loss with only tail-latency spikes (max 3s captured, well under the 10s threshold), I'd like to focus the investigation better. In your experience with similar WSAEWOULDBLOCK popups, what's typically the actual culprit?

  • Firewall session/state table exhaustion dropping the SYN silently?
  • NAT translation delays or stateful inspection stalls?
  • Idle TCP session cleanup hitting mid-connect (some firewalls kill half-open connections aggressively)?
  • SAProuter hop in the path (if applicable) adding a failure point?
  • MTU / MSS mismatch causing SYN fragmentation drops?
  • Or something else that psping wouldn't catch because it's testing a fresh handshake each time?

The reason I'm probing this is my psping baseline literally simulates the same TCP handshake SAP GUI does, and it doesn't reproduce the failure — which makes me think there's a stateful or session-level condition at play that simple synthetic probes can't capture. Curious if you've encountered this before and what specifically to look for on the firewall side.

Any pointers on what to ask our network/security team to check would be gold. Thanks again!

entry.title: Re: Newly created Workbench Transport Requests are not vi...
entry.content:
Hello ,

Thank you for the details you have provided. Based on the information shared, it appears that you have encountered the issue described in the following KBA:

KBA 3191623 - Only 100 transport requests are visible in Fiori Launchpad Designer
(The listbox is limited to displaying 100 transport requests)

Please find below the available workarounds:

---

Workaround 1: Set a Default Transport Request via SU01/SU02

You can pre-configure a default transport request for the affected user to bypass the 100-item limit in the popup.

Steps:
1. Open transaction SU2 (or SU01 → enter the affected user → click Change if applying as administrator)
2. Navigate to the Parameters tab
3. Add a new entry:
- Parameter ID: /UI2/WD_TRKORR_CONF
- Parameter Value: <TR number, e.g. DEVK900123>
4. Save the changes
5. Open /UI2/FLPD_CONF → make a catalog change → confirm the TR is pre-selected in the transport popup

Note: Once your configuration work is complete, please remember to remove this parameter from SU2/SU01 to prevent it from affecting future saves.

For further reference: Setting Default Transport Request Information from Back-End | SAP Help Portal 

---

Workaround 2: Release or Delete Old Transport Requests

Reducing the number of open TRs will also resolve the visibility issue.

To release a TR:
1. Open SE09 and select the relevant TR
2. Release all open tasks (sub-level) first — all tasks must be released before the TR header can be released
3. Release the TR header using the release button
4. The released TR will be moved to the STMS import queue for the target system

To delete an unreleased TR (if it is no longer needed):
1. Open SE09 or SE10 and select the relevant TR
2. Ensure the TR and its tasks have not yet been released — deletion is only possible for unreleased requests
3. Select Delete from the menu
4. The system will remove all associated data from the internal tables

---

Please let us know if you have any questions or require further assistance.

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Unifying the Digital Foundation: A Learner’s Guide to Software Landscape Consolidation
entry.content:
Unifying the Digital Foundation: A Learner’s Guide to Software Landscape Consolidation

1. Introduction: The Concept of a Unified Software Landscape

In the discipline of Enterprise Architecture, "Software Landscape Consolidation" is the strategic process of unifying fragmented, heterogeneous systems into a single, streamlined digital core. Imagine a global corporation that, through years of growth, has ended up with fifty disparate ways of tracking a single sale; consolidation brings these "silos" into one synchronized version of the truth.

Historically, in the 1990s and early 2000s, companies were tethered by hardware limitations. Processing the sheer volume of global data in a single system was technologically impossible. Today, the landscape has shifted. The exponential scalability of the cloud, combined with SAP’s Unicode-based suites and specialized Country Versions, has removed the technical barriers to unification. However, a modern architect must also account for new complexities: geopolitical disruptions and strict data localization laws often require a more nuanced, regionalized approach rather than a single global instance.

Instance Strategy: A high-level decision-making framework used by executives to evaluate software options and determine the optimal number of system "instances" required to maximize business value while balancing local needs with global efficiency.

While these concepts may seem rooted in IT, the primary drivers are financial and operational. As we will see, a fragmented landscape is not just a technical nuisance—it is a significant drain on corporate capital.

2. The Business Case: Why Consolidation Matters for the Bottom Line

Most large enterprises are "accidental architectures," built through years of mergers and acquisitions. Each new business unit often brings its own legacy software—a silo that doesn't communicate with the rest of the organization. These silos inflate the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), obscure global reporting, and paralyze decision-making.

The financial incentive for transformation is immense. Research and benchmarks indicate that IT cost savings from ERP instance consolidation can reach an aggregate of 25%. By eliminating redundant platforms and centralizing support, companies can move funds away from "keeping the lights on" and toward strategic innovation.

Potential Operational Savings | Operational Area | Expected Cost Reduction (%) | | :--- | :--- | | IT Operations | 20% | | End-User Services | 15% | | Application Development | 10% | | Maintenance and Administration | 8% |

Saving money on IT infrastructure is a critical first step, but it is only half the battle. To unlock the full 25% savings potential, an organization must look beyond the servers and address the way people actually work.

3. Process Harmonization: The "Secret Sauce" of Efficiency

System consolidation is considered "suboptimal" if it is not paired with Process Harmonization. Harmonization is the act of aligning business workflows—such as "order-to-cash" or "procure-to-pay"—so they are consistent across global offices.

To understand why this is the "secret sauce," consider the typical cost distribution of enterprise systems:

  • Hardware & Software: 40%
  • System & Data Management: 12%
  • Development, Support, and Training: 42%
  • Other: 8%

The largest expenditure—the 42% dedicated to development and support—is driven by custom variants. When every local office demands its own unique version of a process, support costs skyrocket. Harmonization minimizes these variants, allowing for a leaner, more agile support model. Successful harmonization requires three pillars:

  • Executive Sponsorship: High-level leaders must champion the "global first" mindset.
  • Governance: Disciplined rules to prevent "requirement creep" and unauthorized customization.
  • Change Management: Clear communication to help the workforce transition to standardized workflows.

While harmonization provides the strategic "what," the template strategy serves as the structural "how," bridging the gap between business vision and technical reality.

4. Navigating the Options: Template Strategies and Models

In Enterprise Architecture, we often visualize a hierarchy: Strategy -> Process -> Infrastructure. Your choice of landscape model must reflect this pyramid. You must balance the drive for standardization against the legitimate need for local flexibility.

There are four primary models for structuring your landscape:

  1. Single Instance: One system for the entire global enterprise. This offers the highest standardization but requires massive coordination.
  2. Virtual Single Instance: A "full template" approach where multiple production systems exist but are strictly governed by a single master design.
  3. Template + Multiple Development Systems: A "partial template" where a core design is shared, but local offices use separate development environments for regional additions.
  4. Multiple Development Systems: This offers maximum local flexibility but is the highest-risk model. Without disciplined governance, it inevitably leads to divergence, where local systems become so unique they undermine the very benefits of consolidation.

To manage these complexities, SAP recommends establishing detailed governance and utilizing tools like SAP Solution Manager Cross-Landscape Distribution (XLD) to automate the distribution of configurations across the environment.

When executing these strategies, organizations typically follow one of three scenarios:

  1. "Winner Takes It All": A larger entity's system is forced upon a smaller one. Pros: Speed and cost avoidance. Cons: Can be politically divisive and ignore superior processes in the smaller unit.
  2. "Best of Both Worlds": A merger of equals where the best processes from both are "cherry-picked." Pros: High-quality process design. Cons: Extremely high complexity and risk of "decision deadlock."
  3. "Cluster Selection": Systems are consolidated around autonomous units or regions. Pros: Balances local needs with corporate structure. Cons: Reduced global transparency compared to a single instance.

5. The Intelligent Enterprise: AI and Cloud as Accelerators

Modern platforms like SAP S/4HANA Cloud (Private and Public Editions) and Business Data Cloud (BDC) have changed the game by embedding intelligence directly into the digital core. Consolidation is no longer just about "cleaning up"; it is about enabling an Intelligent Enterprise.

By embedding Business AI into a consolidated landscape, organizations gain three transformative advantages:

  • Automating Tasks: Offloading repetitive, low-value work to AI agents.
  • Predicting Trends: Utilizing unified global data to forecast market shifts.
  • Enhanced Decision-Making: Shifting the corporate focus from "data collection" to "data-driven action."

Note: SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) SAP BTP is the cornerstone of the "Clean Core" philosophy. It allows companies to keep their main ERP system standardized and easily upgradeable while providing a separate, flexible layer to build custom innovations and integrations.

These tools turn a technical consolidation project into a distinct competitive advantage, allowing a company to respond to market changes with a speed that fragmented competitors cannot match.

6. Conclusion: From Strategy to Execution

The journey to a unified landscape is complex, but the cost of inaction—missed efficiency, visibility, and growth—is far higher. In the world of Enterprise Architecture, indecision is worse than a decision.

To move from strategy to execution, follow this structured framework:

  1. Define Objectives: Clearly state if the goal is cost reduction, global visibility, or digital scalability.
  2. Scenario Development: Build up to four viable blueprints for the landscape.
  3. Evaluation Criteria: Weighted ranking based on TCO, complexity, and change impact.
  4. Collective Vote: Ensure IT and Business leaders are in total alignment on the final design.

Key Takeaways Checklist:

  • [ ] Target 25% Savings: ERP instance consolidation is a major financial lever for the CFO.
  • [ ] Standardize via "Clean Core": Use SAP BTP to innovate while keeping the core system simple.
  • [ ] Beware of Divergence: Multiple development systems require strict governance to avoid returning to "silos."
  • [ ] Address Localization: Account for geopolitical and data residency needs in your initial design.
  • [ ] Business Ownership: Consolidation must be a business-led transformation, not just an IT task.

entry.title: Building a Production-Ready Custom ATC Check: Enforcing ABAP Unit Coverage
entry.content:
Introduction

Many development teams encourage ABAP Unit adoption, but enforcing minimum test coverage is still a challenge.

During a recent project, I needed a way to prevent the release of new developments that did not meet a minimum coverage threshold.

Since I couldn't find a standard ATC check that fulfilled this requirement, I decided to build a custom ATC validation.

The final solution became a lightweight and configurable quality gate that can be integrated directly into existing SCI and ATC processes.

Inspiration

Before starting the implementation, I analyzed several open-source ATC extensions, especially the SAP Code Pal for ABAP project.

Code Pal is an excellent reference for understanding how custom checks can be integrated into the ATC framework and how configurable validations can be implemented.

However, my goal was not to build a generic quality framework. Instead, I wanted a lightweight solution focused on a single requirement: enforcing a minimum ABAP Unit test coverage threshold.

For this reason, I implemented a simplified check that leverages the standard SCI/ATC framework and ABAP Unit coverage APIs while remaining easy to maintain and extend.

Understanding the Solution

The solution is composed of three main components:

  • A custom ATC category
  • A custom ATC check
  • A configurable SCI/ATC variant

The execution flow is illustrated below:

SCI / ATC
      ↓
Custom Check
      ↓
ABAP Unit Coverage Analysis
      ↓
Threshold Validation
      ↓
ATC Finding

Creating a Custom Check Category

The first step is to create a custom category that will group all checks.

Create a class that inherits from CL_CI_CATEGORY_ROOT.

METHOD constructor.
  super->constructor( ).

  description = TEXT-t01.
  category    = me->ci_category.
ENDMETHOD.

This category will later appear in the SCI administration screen and will act as a container for all custom validations.

Creating the Check Class

Next, create the actual validation check by inheriting from CL_CI_TEST_ROOT.

In the constructor, define the basic metadata of the check:

  • Description
  • Category
  • Display position
  • Version
  • Error messages
METHOD constructor.
  super->constructor( ).

  description = `Minimum ABAP Unit Coverage`.
  category    = `Your Custom Category`.
  position    = `010`.
  version    += 1.

  APPEND VALUE #(
           sign   = `I`
           option = `EQ`
           low    = `1PRG`
         ) TO me->typelist.

  APPEND VALUE #(
           test = me->myname
           code = `CI001`
           kind = me->c_error
           text = 'Insufficient coverage: &1% (min. &2%)'
         ) TO me->scimessages.
ENDMETHOD.

Once activated and registered in SCI, the category and the check should appear in the SCI management screen.

New checks

Category and new check

Adding Configuration Parameters

One of the main goals of this implementation was to make the validation configurable.

Instead of hardcoding values, the minimum coverage threshold and object filters are maintained through the SCI variant.

To achieve this, I redefined the method IF_CI_TEST~QUERY_ATTRIBUTES.

This allows us to build a custom parameter screen such as:

  • Minimum Coverage (%)
  • Created After
  • Object Type
  • BAdI Implementations Only
  • RAP Behavior Implementations Only
METHOD if_ci_test~query_attributes.
  DATA(attributes) = VALUE sci_atttab(
    ...
  ).

  DATA(break) =
    cl_ci_query_attributes=>generic(
      p_name       = me->myname
      p_title      = me->description
      p_attributes = attributes
      p_display    = p_display ).
ENDMETHOD.

The resulting configuration screen looks like this:

Screen

Persisting Variant Parameters

The values entered by users need to be stored and restored whenever the variant is executed.

This is done by redefining the methods GET_ATTRIBUTES and PUT_ATTRIBUTES.

METHOD get_attributes.
  EXPORT attributes_filled = attributes_filled
    TO DATA BUFFER p_attributes.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD put_attributes.
  IMPORT attributes_filled = attributes_filled
    FROM DATA BUFFER p_attributes.
ENDMETHOD.

Implementing the Validation Logic

The heart of every ATC check is the RUN method.

This method is executed for every analyzed object.

METHOD run.
  CHECK me->program_name IS NOT INITIAL.
  CHECK me->get( ) = me->c_true.

  CHECK me->object_type IN me->attributes_filled-object_type.
  CHECK me->is_after_minimum_creation_date( ) >=
        me->attributes_filled-date_from.

  DATA(percentage) = me->get_coverage( ).

  IF percentage < me->attributes_filled-coverage_minimun.

    inform(
      p_sub_obj_type = me->c_type_include
      p_sub_obj_name = me->program_name
      p_kind         = me->c_error
      p_test         = me->myname
      p_code         = `CI001`
      p_param_1      = |{ percentage NUMBER = USER }|
      p_param_2      = |{ me->attributes_filled-coverage_minimun NUMBER = USER }|
    ).
  ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

Measuring ABAP Unit Coverage

One of the most interesting parts of this implementation was discovering that ABAP Unit coverage can be measured programmatically.

The class CL_AUCV_TASK provides the necessary APIs.

METHOD get_coverage.
  DATA(aunit) =
    cl_aucv_task=>create(
      i_measure_coverage = abap_true ).

  ...

  percentage =
    branch_coverage->get_percentage( ).
ENDMETHOD.

This allows the ATC check to compare the actual coverage with the threshold configured in the SCI variant.

Executing the Check

After creating an SCI variant and selecting the custom check, simply configure the desired threshold.

For example:

  • Created After = 01.01.2025
  • Minimum Coverage = 85%
  • Object Type = CLAS

First, let's measure the ABAP Unit coverage of a sample object:

ABAP Unit

When ATC is executed, findings are generated whenever the object's coverage is below the required percentage.

ATC Result

Lessons Learned

  • ATC is far more extensible than it initially appears.
  • Custom checks can be used to implement real governance requirements.
  • ABAP Unit coverage can be measured programmatically.
  • Configurable checks simplify gradual adoption.
  • Quality gates are an effective way to increase test adoption.

Final Thoughts

By combining the SCI framework, ATC extensibility and ABAP Unit coverage APIs, it is possible to build lightweight but powerful quality controls directly inside the SAP development process.

In addition to ATC execution, I also integrated this validation into the transport release process, ensuring that new developments cannot be released when they fail to meet the required coverage threshold.

Have you ever implemented a custom ATC validation in your landscape? Feel free to share your experience in the comments.

entry.title: Reading Between the Lines: Under-Documented Fiori Elements Capabilities in SAPUI5 1.149
entry.content:
Every SAPUI5 release ships more than its release notes admit. Between the headline features sits a quieter layer: public, load-bearing capability that is barely documented. You usually meet it while stepping through a -dbg.js at the wrong hour. What follows is a reading of that layer in Fiori Elements 1.149, taken from the framework source. The theme is not a feature list. It is a shift in where behavior belongs, and increasingly that place is the CAP backend.

The center of gravity moved to the backend

The old reflex was to close Fiori Elements gaps in a controller extension. The recent additions are annotation-first: the frontend honors what the service declares.

Event-driven SideEffects (@since 1.148) are the clearest case. A backend event travels over a WebSocket channel and the visible page re-reads exactly the targets the service named. No polling, no refresh code. On the CAP side this is one plugin, @cap-js-community/websocket (github.com/cap-js-community/websocket). Enable both protocols on the service and model a side-effect event:

// srv/service.cds
@ws
@odata
service TravelService {
  entity Travel as projection on my.Travel;

  event travelStatusChanged {
    sideEffectSource : String; // the changed instance path
  }
}

// The plugin derives these automatically from the OData path:
// @Common.WebSocketBaseURL : 'ws/travel'
// @Common.WebSocketChannel #sideEffects : 'sideeffects'

annotate TravelService.Travel with @(
  Common.SideEffects #StatusPush : {
    SourceEvents     : [ 'travelStatusChanged' ],
    TargetProperties : [ 'TravelStatus_code', 'TotalPrice' ]
  }
);

Emit from a handler and every open page re-reads the named targets. cds.queued couples the push to transaction success:

// srv/service.js
module.exports = (srv) => {
  srv.after('UPDATE', 'Travel', async (travel, req) => {
    const svc = cds.queued(srv); // fire only if the tx commits
    await svc.tx(req).emit('travelStatusChanged', {
      sideEffectSource: `/Travel(ID=${travel.ID},IsActiveEntity=true)`
    });
  });
};

Read that carefully: the model, not the app, owns the freshness contract. The frontend hook exists, but its default is to do the sensible thing and get out of the way.

The same instinct runs through AI field recommendations (@since 1.139). The service marks fields with Common.RecommendationRole; the framework renders the placeholders, the value-help grouping, and the accept-all dialog.

annotate TravelService.Travel with {
  CustomerID  @Common.RecommendationRole : #Input;
  Description @Common.RecommendationRole : #Output;
};

What it does not ship is an inference backend. isEnabled returns false and fetchRecommendations is yours:

// ext/Travel.controller.ts (controller extension)
override isEnabled() { return true; }
override async fetchRecommendations(contexts) {
  return contexts.map((ctx) => ({
    context: ctx,
    AIRecommendedFieldPath: 'Description',
    AIRecommendedFieldValue: 'Business trip to Walldorf'
  }));
}

A deliberate seam: the interaction is standardized, the intelligence is delegated. The framework conceded the boring 80% and asks only for the model call.

Collaboration stopped being a bolt-on

Collaborative draft (@since 1.141) lets several users co-author one draft with live field locking over a PCP WebSocket. There is no manifest switch. It activates when the service exposes a draft root, a share action, and a WebSocket base URL. On ABAP RAP that comes for free. On CAP Node.js it did not exist until cap-collaborative-draft (github.com/sleibach/cap-collaborative-draft), a cds-plugin that reproduces the full OData contract. Two annotations on the service entity:

// srv/service.cds
service OrderService {
  @odata.draft.enabled
  @CollaborativeDraft.enabled : true
  entity Orders as projection on my.Orders { *, Items }
}

The plugin injects @Common.DraftRoot.ShareAction, the WebSocket annotations, and the presence and lock contract. It leans on the same @cap-js-community/websocket plugin as a peer dependency. Application code stays out of the protocol and subscribes only where it has domain intent, for example turning an invite into a notification:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

cds.on('collab-draft:shareInvite', ({ draftUUID, invitedBy, users }) => {
  for (const user of users) sendInviteEmail({ to: user.UserID, draftUUID, invitedBy });
});

On the UI5 side the reuse layer follows the same principle. ExtensionAPI.collaborativeDraft exposes sendPropertyValuesChange, sendLockChange, and their siblings only for the one case that needs them: your code mutating the shared draft and having to tell the other editors. The absence of configuration is the design, not an oversight.

The environment-gated integrations behave the same way. Publishing a page as an Insights card, feeding screen context to Joule, Microsoft Teams contact actions: none ask for a flag. They light up when the host library is present and stay silent otherwise. Stop guarding them with conditionals and let capability detection work.

Building blocks are a public composition API

The sap.fe.macros.* controls graduated from template internals to a supported way to assemble custom pages. In 1.145 an interface hierarchy (ITableColumn, ITableAction, ITableActionOrGroup) formalized what may nest inside a Table, the kind of change that only happens when an API is meant to be extended from outside. MacroAPI implements IFormContent, so a macro drops into a hand-built form:

<macros:Table id="Travels" metaPath="@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem" contextPath="/Travel">
  <macros:columns>
    <macros:Column key="profit" header="{i18n>profit}" anchor="TotalPrice" placement="After">
      <template><Text text="{= ${Revenue} - ${Cost} }"/></template>
    </macros:Column>
  </macros:columns>
</macros:Table>

And where you once redrew a whole table to change a column, addColumn / removeColumn mutate it at runtime (still experimental; treat the label as a promise about stability, not capability). Rarely mentioned: RichTextEditor and RichTextEditorWithMetadata have been public building blocks since 1.117. If you embedded a third-party editor to bind a long-text property, the platform already had one.

The manifest still hides the most leverage

Much of what teams rebuild by hand is a flag away: multi-view list reports, mass edit with a custom save hook, in-place object-page editing, a per-context paginator rule. All declarative, all in converters/ManifestSettings.ts.

"controlConfiguration": {
  "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem": {
    "tableSettings": { "enableMassEdit": true }
  }
}

That file is also a warning: almost no @since tags, and a few switches are labeled temporary in the source (disableCollaborationDraft carries the comment “will be deleted again”). Read the comments as a boundary between what you may lean on and what you may not.

What this adds up to

The pattern is consistent. Model behavior where the data lives, let the framework ship the interaction and ask you only for the domain core, and let features turn on when their environment is ready. Two CAP plugins, @cap-js-community/websocket and cap-collaborative-draft, close the gap that used to make these features ABAP-only.

The practical discipline: before writing the extension, check whether an annotation, a manifest flag, a public building block, or a CAP plugin already does it. In 1.149, more often than you would expect, one does.

Version anchors were verified against the 1.149 framework source. Anything marked experimental or internal can change without notice. The two CAP plugins are community projects under active development; pin exact versions before you depend on them.

entry.title: How to Set Up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to Consume APM eIoT Rule-Based Events
entry.content:
How to Set Up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub to Consume APM eIoT Rule-Based Events

Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) provides powerful condition-based monitoring of asset health through Rules based on embedded IoT. These rules continuously evaluate sensor data and operational parameters against defined conditions. When a condition is met, the rule can generate an event as its output result.

With SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, these events can be routed to your own applications — enabling you to build custom handlers and business logic that react to asset health changes in real time. Whether you want to trigger maintenance workflows, send notifications, or feed data into analytics pipelines, Event Hub provides the infrastructure to consume these events reliably.

In this blog post, you will learn step by step how to set up SAP Cloud Application Event Hub in your BTP landscape to receive events from APM Rules based on embedded IoT. By the end, you will have a fully configured event routing pipeline from APM through Event Hub to a consumer of your choice — either SAP Integration Suite (recommended) or your own BTP application.

What You Will Achieve

  • A configured Event Hub application connected to your APM system
  • A subscription to the three APM Rules based on embedded IoT event types
  • A working event flow from APM through Event Hub to your consumer — SAP Integration Suite or a custom BTP application

APM Rules based on embedded IoT Event Types

APM publishes three event types through Event Hub:

Event Type Description

sap.apm.MonitoringRule.Run.v1Triggered when a monitoring rule condition is met
sap.apm.DerivationRule.Run.v1Triggered when a derivation rule produces a result
sap.apm.AggregationRule.Run.v1Triggered when an aggregation rule produces a result

> Tip: You can find the full event specification on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

 

 

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have:

  • A BTP global account with administrative access
  • Your organization has completed onboarding to Unified Customer Landscape (UCL)
  • Entitlements for SAP Cloud Application Event Hub are available in your global account

> Note: If you haven't onboarded to UCL yet, this is a prerequisite for consuming business events. Please complete the UCL onboarding first. Refer to the SAP Cloud Application Event Hub documentation for details.

> Important: When onboarding to UCL, ensure that SPII notifications are not enabled. APM does not support SPII notifications yet. If SPII notifications are enabled during UCL onboarding, the integration will not work correctly.

 

 

Overview of the Setup Flow

Before you begin, decide which consumer will receive the events. The rest of the flow is largely the same either way:

  • SAP Integration Suite (recommended) — appears automatically in your System Landscape once entitled. No manual service instance or system registration is needed. Suite users skip Steps 4 and 5.
  • Your own BTP application — requires creating an Event Hub service instance (Step 4) and registering the app in the System Landscape (Step 5).

The overall process has five main stages:

1.  BTP Setup — Assign entitlements, subscribe to the Event Hub application, and assign the admin role (Steps 1–3, required for both consumer types)
2.  Custom Consumer Application Setup (custom BTP application only) — Create the Event Hub service instance and register your app in the System Landscape (Steps 4–5). Integration Suite users skip this stage
3.  UCL Formation — Connect APM, Event Hub, and your consumer (Integration Suite or custom app) in a Formation of type Eventing Between SAP Cloud Systems (Step 6)
4. Integration Dependencies & Subscriptions — Define which event types your consumer receives and activate them in the Event Hub UI (Steps 7–8)
5. APM Rule Configuration — Enable External Events as an output on your APM rules and verify execution (Step 9)
