entry.title: Controlling the View: How Access Control and Business Services in SAP Cloud ALM Protect Your Monitor
entry.content:
The Problem with Open Access

 

Before Access Control was introduced, any user with a general monitoring role in SAP Cloud ALM could access all landscape objects — every service, every system, every message. For small teams managing a handful of systems, this was acceptable. For larger organisations with multiple business units, subsidiaries, or compliance requirements, it created a real data governance gap.

 

The solution is Attribute-based Access Control (ABAC) — a mechanism that ties visibility of landscape objects directly to user assignments, not just roles.
 

What Is Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM?

Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM allows administrators to restrict which **services**, **systems**, and **business services** are visible to which users.
Once activated, users will only see the landscape objects assigned to them through an **Access Control List (ACL)**. Users not assigned to any ACL will see nothing.

Important: Access Control activation is irreversible. Once switched on, it cannot be turned off. Careful preparation — including creating ACLs and assigning users before activation — is essential.
 

The Role of Business Services

A Business Service in Integration & Exception Monitoring is a a logical grouping that maps technical integration artefacts to a recognisable business context — for example, showing only specific iFlows from an SAP Integration Suite system, or specific interfaces from an AIF setup for a business transactions like Order-to-cash or Hire-to-Retire.

This makes Business Services the natural vehicle for **data-level access control**. Rather than granting a user access to an entire system, an administrator can:

1. Create a Business Service that includes only the relevant systems and interfaces
2. Apply filters (e.g., iFlow name, interface name, object type) to limit the data visible within those systems
3. Assign the Business Service to an ACL
4. Assign the user to that ACL

The result: the user sees only the monitoring data relevant to their scope — nothing more.
 

How to Set It Up

Step 1 — Assign the Access Controller Role

Designate one or more users responsible for managing ACLs. These users need the **Landscape Access Controller** role.

Step 2 — Create Business Services (if needed)

In Integration & Exception Monitoring, navigate to **Configuration → Business Services**. Create a Business Service, add the relevant systems, and define filters for the interfaces, iFlows, or object types that should be visible within it.

Step 3 — Create Access Control Lists

In Landscape Management, navigate to **Configuration → Attribute-based Access Control → Access Control List Maintenance**. Create one or more ACLs, each with:
- A name and description
- Assigned landscape objects (services, systems, and/or business services)
- Assigned users, with read or edit privileges

Step 4 — Test Before Activating

Use the **Test Access Control** feature to restrict individual users temporarily and verify they see only what is intended — before activating globally.

Step 5 — Activate Access Control

Once all ACLs are in place and tested, activate Access Control globally under Configuration → Attribute-based Access Control → Access Control Settings
 
Key Behaviors to Be Aware Of
- Business Services and systems are independent Granting a user access to a Business Service does not automatically grant access to the underlying systems. The systems must be explicitly included in an ACL if direct system-level access is needed.
- Rule-based vs. fixed restrictions ACLs support both rule-based restrictions (covering all landscape objects matching certain attributes, including new ones added in future) and fixed restrictions (covering one specific object by ID). Rule-based restrictions are the recommended approach for large or evolving landscapes.
- Super-users can be exempted Users who need unrestricted access across the entire landscape can be exempted from ACL restrictions individually, without the need for all-encompassing access control lists.
 

Why This Matters for Organizations

 

For organizations operating in regulated industries, managing multi-region landscapes, or simply enforcing the principle of least privilege, this combination of Access Control and Business Services provides:

- Data separation across teams and business units
- Operational clarity — teams see only what is relevant to them, reducing noise and the risk of acting on data outside their scope
- Scalability — rule-based ACLs automatically cover new landscape objects as the landscape grows
 

Further Reading

For a complete guide on setting up Access Control and working with Business Services, refer to the official SAP Help Portal documentation:

SAP Cloud ALM – Access Control

For a step-by-step walkthrough on creating Business Services in Integration Monitoring
 
Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM is a deliberate, one-way gate. Plan it carefully, test it thoroughly, and once it is in place, your organization's monitoring data will be exactly as visible — and no more — as it needs to be.

entry.title: Re: SAP ABAP training advice please.
entry.content:
Hello Andy,

Thank you for your message and for sharing your background. With around 20 years of SAP experience in manufacturing, you already have a very strong functional foundation. This is especially valuable because ABAP development in an SAP environment benefits greatly from a solid understanding of business processes.

In my view, Java or Python knowledge is not a prerequisite for getting started with ABAP. A general understanding of programming logic is certainly helpful, and your experience as a CNC programmer may give you a good starting point. ABAP is its own programming language with its own concepts, and it can be learned step by step.

I would recommend starting with the ABAP fundamentals: data types, internal tables, Open SQL, modularization, debugging, and simple reports. Since your employer is currently using an ECC 6.0 system, classic ABAP knowledge is still highly relevant. Later on, you can build on this with topics such as ALV, BAPIs, BADIs, user exits, and the Enhancement Framework.

A structured beginner-level training would be a good way to start. You can find a suitable training here: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/acquiring-core-abap-skills

In addition, I would suggest doing small hands-on exercises in an SAP system as early as possible. ABAP is best learned by debugging, reading existing programs, and creating small reports yourself.

Wishing you every success as you get started with ABAP.

entry.title: Ask Your IT Landscape Anything: A Hands-On Guide to SAP LeanIX's Built-In MCP Server
entry.content:
Most enterprise architecture tools have the same problem: the data is there, but getting to it takes sometimes more time than you would like. That changes with MCP (Model Context Protocol). SAP LeanIX now ships with a built-in MCP server, which means you can connect any compatible AI assistant to your workspace and simply ask questions in plain language.

In this post I'll show you how to set it up and walk through eight real use cases which you can leverage at any stage of knowledge. Whether you're an enterprise architect, a program manager, or a business stakeholder, this post has something for you.

 

What is MCP and why does it matter for EA?

Think of MCP as a universal connector, it lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources in a standardised way. It was introduced by Anthropic in late 2024 and has since become the de facto standard for giving AI models access to real-world systems.

For LeanIX, this means your AI assistant can reach directly into your workspace, call the right tools, combine results from multiple sources, and give you a structured, contextual answer or even do tasks for you within your workspace. And this all from a single question.

The practical impact is that enterprise architects get faster answers, business stakeholders can ask questions without needing platform access or enterprise architecture knowledge, and everyone works from the same live data.

Looking further ahead, MCP is also the foundation for working with AI agents. While AI assistants typically respond to direct user prompts, AI agents extend this concept further. Agents can autonomously plan and execute multiple tool calls, reason over intermediate results, and iteratively work toward a broader goal. MCP provides the standardised interface that enables these interactions.

 

Setup: Three ways to get started

Before anything else, you need an API token from your LeanIX workspace. This token is required for all setup options below.

Go to your LeanIX workspace → click your profile icon (top right) → Administration → API Tokens → + New API Token. Copy the token and keep it somewhere safe because it is only shown once.

Once you have your token, choose how you want to connect:

 

Option A — LeanIX MCP Chat

The LeanIX MCP Chat is a dedicated chat interface built for exactly this purpose. You can access it here: LeanIX MCP Chat

  1. In the chat interface, click MCP-Servers in the bottom left sidebar → Add Server.
  2. Give it a name, select LeanIX as the Transport Type and open your LeanIX workspace in the browser to copy the instance name from the URL (e.g. demo-eu-10.leanix.net) and paste it into the Instance field. It will automatically show you the full MCP endpoint URL it will connect to.
  3. Then paste your API Token into the token field

 

Option B — Claude Desktop

In Claude, go to Settings → Developer → Edit Config and add the following to your claude_desktop_config.json:

{

  "mcpServers": {

    "LeanIX": {

      "type": "http",

      "url": "https://<region>.leanix.net/services/mcp-server/v1/mcp",

      "headers": {

        "Authorization": "Bearer LXT_YOUR_TOKEN”

      }

    }

  }

}

Replace <region> with the regional prefix of your LeanIX workspace URL and YOUR_TOKEN with the LXT_ token you generated.

Then save the file and restart your Claude.

 

Option C – Cursor

The recommended approach is to edit the MCP config file directly. Open (or create) ~/.cursor/mcp.json and add the following:

{

  "mcpServers": {

    "LeanIX": {

      "type": "http",

      "url": "https://<region>.leanix.net/services/mcp-server/v1/mcp",

      "headers": {

        "Authorization": "Bearer LXT_YOUR_TOKEN"

      }

    }

  }

}

Then restart your Cursor.

 

8 use cases that show what this actually looks like

Getting a quick lay of the land

New to a project or just need a fast briefing? Instead of clicking through multiple views, just ask.

"Give me an overview of our IT landscape - how many applications, initiatives and business capabilities do we have?"

In seconds, the AI called get_overview and returned a full count: 153 applications, 40 initiatives, 89 business capabilities, 965 fact sheets in total. A useful first question for anyone onboarding to a new account or preparing for a stakeholder meeting.

 

Catching end-of-life risk before it catches you

Tracking application lifecycle across a large portfolio is one of those tasks that's always urgent and never quite done. This query makes it immediate.

"Which applications in my portfolio are reaching end-of-life within the next 12 months?"

The AI combined get_applications and get_fact_sheet_details to return a prioritised list with EOL dates, hosting type, business criticality, and owner information. Notice that two tools were called automatically in sequence. You didn't need to specify how to get the answer, only what you wanted to know.

 

 

Keeping transformation programs on track

Portfolio managers and program leads often struggle to get a quick read on the status of multiple parallel initiatives. One question covers it.

"What is the current status of our transformation initiatives and which milestones are still outstanding?"

The response broke down all 40 initiatives by lifecycle status, highlighted the key programs with their completion percentages, and surfaced the single upcoming named milestone like Wave II finalized on 2026-11-01. The kind of summary that would sometimes take a reporting cycle to produce.

 

Understanding the blast radius before you act

Before decommissioning any core system, you need to know what depends on it. This is where the multi-tool capability of MCP really earns its place.

“If we were to decommission one of our core applications - which other applications depend on it and would be affected?” follow up question: "SAP ECC, extended impact please - applications, interfaces and business capabilities"

The AI chained eight separate tool calls with searching by name, pulling fact sheet details, traversing relations and produced a full impact map: the core ECC module applications (FI, CO, MM, HCM), their downstream dependencies, the business capabilities at risk across Finance, HR and Manufacturing, and a clear summary of what breaks if ECC goes dark. 

 

 

Closing data quality gaps at scale

Missing business owners are one of the most common data quality problems in any EA repository. Instead of manually hunting them down, you can use the AI to build and distribute a targeted survey.

"Create a survey for all application owners to fill in missing business owner information"

The AI identified 144 applications with no Business Owner subscription, drafted a complete survey proposal including title, target audience, duration, and four specific questions and asked for confirmation before creating anything.

 

 

Turning architecture insights into a clear next step

LeanIX generates architecture insights automatically based on patterns in your data. Getting to them quickly, and knowing which one to act on first, is where the AI adds value in this use case.

"What are our current architecture insights and which has the highest priority for our next review?"

The insight surfaced a functional overlap between Concur and TravelPerk in the travel and expense domain, with a clear rationale for prioritization: Concur reaches end-of-life in eight months, making this time-sensitive.

 

Finding what you don't know

Not every query returns a clean list of results, and that's useful too. When asked about GPL-licensed open source components across the portfolio, the AI ran five consecutive license searches and found nothing. Not because the problem doesn't exist, but because no SBOMs had been attached to the applications.

"Which applications in our portfolio use GPL-licensed open source libraries that could create legal risk?"

The response was honest about the coverage gap, explained exactly why the results were empty, and offered three concrete next steps: broaden the search to LGPL variants, drill down into specific high-risk apps, or set up automated monitoring.

 

Enforcing data quality standards

When standards slip, broken seals accumulate quietly across your portfolio. Rather than hunting them down fact sheet by fact sheet, you can ask once and get a complete, prioritized view of every application that is out of compliance.

          "Show me all applications that currently have a broken quality seal and explain what's missing"

The AI returned the complete set of 144 applications with a broken quality seal, sorted by last update, each with a direct link to the fact sheet. What stood out was the follow-up: the AI immediately offered three concrete next steps.  

 

What to take away from this

SAP LeanIX's MCP server is not a prototype or a workaround but it is a built-in capability with currently 40+ dedicated tools covering applications, initiatives, architecture decisions, tech stacks, surveys, SBOM data, and more. For business stakeholders, this means getting answers without needing platform access or Enterprise Architecture knowledge. For architects and IT teams, it means faster analysis with less manual effort. And for anyone responsible for data quality, it means the gaps in your EA repository become visible and actionable immediately.

 

Find the list of available MCP tools here:

Can actively modify data (write):

  • create_survey — create a new survey (poll) in LeanIX for a set of fact sheets.
  • create_survey_reminder — create/send a reminder for an existing survey run.
  • change_survey_status — change the status of a survey run (poll run).
  • create_architecture_decision — create an architecture decision from a template.
  • create_tech_stack — create a custom technology stack in the workspace.
  • delete_architecture_decision — permanently delete an architecture decision (irreversible).
  • update_architecture_decision — update an existing architecture decision's fields, status, or linked fact sheets.

Read-only / consume data (no modification):

  • get_fact_sheet_types — list all fact sheet types.
  • get_overview — workspace overview (counts, facets).
  • get_fact_sheet_details — fetch full details for up to 25 fact sheet IDs.
  • search_fact_sheet_by_name — find fact sheets by exact name.
  • text_to_fact_sheets — natural-language search for fact sheets (filter generation).
  • get_applications — search/filter Application fact sheets.
  • get_application_components — retrieve SBOM/library components for an application.
  • search_components_by_purl — search library components by PURL.
  • batch_search_components_by_purls — batch PURL lookups.
  • get_architecture_decisions — list architecture decisions.
  • get_architecture_templates — list architecture decision templates.
  • get_architecture_template_components — retrieve components of a template.
  • get_reports — list stored reports.
  • get_diagrams — list diagrams.
  • get_diagram_by_id — fetch a diagram by UUID.
  • get_initiatives — list initiatives with relations and lifecycle.
  • get_milestones_by_fact_sheet_ids — get initiative milestones.
  • get_insights — retrieve LeanIX architecture guidance insights.
  • search_transformations_tool — search transformations (projects/executions).
  • get_transformation_types_tool — list transformation type definitions.
  • search_users — find users in the workspace.
  • get_user_subscriptions — list fact sheets a user is subscribed to.
  • get_fact_sheet_subscriptions — list subscriptions for a fact sheet.
  • get_survey_templates — list poll/questionnaire templates.
  • get_survey_recipients — list who is involved in a survey run.
  • get_survey_results — retrieve survey results.
  • get_reminders — list reminders for a survey run.
  • search_survey_runs — list/search survey runs.
  • get_all_tech_stacks — list discovered tech stacks.
  • get_tech_stack_details — get details for a tech stack.
  • preview_tech_stack_matches — preview PURL matches for tech-stack rules.
  • get_architecture_decision_as_markdown — export an architecture decision as markdown with YAML front matter.
  • get_architecture_decision — fetch a single architecture decision by UUID.
  • count_fact_sheet_relations — count relations from a source fact sheet type, grouped by target.
  • search_components_by_license — search library components by license name (e.g. MIT, GPL, Apache).

Have questions or want to share your own use cases? Drop a comment below! I'd love to hear how others are using LeanIX with AI.

entry.title: Re: PLANNED REPLENISHMENT IN EWM
entry.content:
@Goddi_Hadil check if you have enough stock in warehouse. Also check the entries (min /max qty in binmat and also the min. repl qty in MAT1). If min qty + min repl qty > max qty the system wont create any warehouse task for replenishment. You should also check the stock removal control.

 

@former_member677004 thank you for your straight explanation!
